canonical_examples.analysis: The thermostat example separates single-loop from double-loop learning. A thermostat that detects a room is too cold and turns on heat corrects an error inside a fixed governing variable: maintain the preset temperature. Double-loop learning would ask whether the preset itself, the comfort standard, the occupancy assumptions, or the energy-use norm should change. The example shows that error correction is not enough when the control variable is itself part of the problem.
canonical_examples.problem: Argyris and Schon's standard thermostat analogy: a device detects that room temperature differs from a preset target and activates heating to restore the target.

canonical_examples.analysis: Argyris uses this case to show how Model I reasoning blocks learning. Managers say they want valid information and commitment, but in practice they unilaterally control the agenda, suppress negative data, and treat disagreement as disloyalty. Single-loop fixes improve presentation, monitoring, or compliance while preserving those governing variables. Double-loop learning surfaces the undiscussable norms, tests whether unilateral control is producing defensive routines, and moves toward Model II values: valid information, free and informed choice, and internal commitment.
canonical_examples.problem: A management team repeatedly fails to learn from project breakdowns because meetings avoid embarrassment, disagreement, and criticism of senior assumptions.

core_claims: Organizations can correct visible errors while preserving the governing variables that produced those errors; such single-loop correction often stabilizes the underlying dysfunction.
The decisive learning question is not only whether action matched intention, but whether the intentions, norms, policies, and problem frames should be altered.
Defensive routines protect individuals and organizations from embarrassment or threat, and thereby make the most important premises undiscussable.
Model I theories-in-use favor unilateral control, winning, suppressing negative feelings, and acting rationally; they predictably produce low-validity information and weak learning.
Model II learning requires publicly testable claims, valid information, free and informed choice, and internal commitment rather than compliance.
Espoused theories often diverge from theories-in-use; reliable diagnosis must infer governing variables from behavior, not from stated values alone.
description: Double-Loop Learning is Chris Argyris and Donald Schon's framework for distinguishing ordinary error correction within existing governing assumptions from deeper learning that questions and revises those assumptions themselves.
failure_modes: Endless assumption-questioning can become avoidance of operational repair; some problems really are single-loop defects in execution.
Practitioners can perform openness rhetorically while preserving Model I control, turning double-loop language into another defensive routine.
Surfacing governing variables without psychological safety or authority to change them can increase cynicism and threat.
The framework can overattribute failure to defensive reasoning when material constraints, incentives, or technical skill gaps are the primary cause.
Double-loop inquiry is slow and socially demanding; using it for every minor variance can exhaust attention and make learning rituals feel punitive.

key_vocabulary.Defensive routine: A practiced pattern that prevents people from experiencing embarrassment or threat while also preventing them from examining the causes of that threat.
key_vocabulary.Double-loop learning: Learning that detects and corrects error by changing the governing variables, norms, policies, or objectives that shaped the original action.
key_vocabulary.Espoused theory: The values, strategies, and assumptions people claim guide their action. Argyris contrasts it with the theory actually revealed in behavior.
key_vocabulary.Governing variables: The values, goals, policies, and assumptions that define what counts as success and constrain acceptable action.
key_vocabulary.Model I: A theory-in-use organized around unilateral control, winning, minimizing negative feelings, and appearing rational. It tends to create defensiveness and low-validity information.
key_vocabulary.Model II: A theory-in-use organized around valid information, free and informed choice, and internal commitment. It is the normative pattern Argyris associates with productive learning.
key_vocabulary.Single-loop learning: Learning that detects and corrects error without changing the governing variables, like a thermostat restoring a preset temperature.
key_vocabulary.Theory-in-use: The implicit action theory that can be inferred from what people actually do, especially under stress or conflict.

name: Double-Loop Learning
operative_procedure: Name the error or persistent mismatch between intended and actual outcomes without yet treating it as an execution defect.
Map the current single-loop response: what correction, control, training, incentive, or monitoring action is being used to restore the existing target?
Elicit the governing variables that make the current response sensible: targets, success criteria, taboos, role assumptions, control preferences, and risk definitions.
Compare espoused theory with theory-in-use by using concrete episodes: what people said they valued versus what their actions protected when stakes rose.
Identify defensive routines and undiscussables: what information is not allowed into the conversation, what embarrassment is being avoided, and who benefits from that avoidance.
Test whether the governing variables are valid by asking what evidence would change them and whether affected actors can challenge them publicly.
Redesign toward Model II conditions: valid information, free and informed choice, joint control of inquiry, and internal commitment to the revised action.
Run a new action experiment and check both loops: whether the immediate error improved and whether the revised governing variables changed behavior under pressure.
scope_and_limits: Double-Loop Learning is strongest for recurring organizational failures, policy contradictions, and interpersonal patterns where ordinary fixes preserve the conditions that create the problem. It is less useful for one-off technical defects, emergencies requiring rapid execution, or settings where participants cannot safely examine governing assumptions.
source_summary: Authored from model knowledge of Chris Argyris and Donald Schon's work on organizational learning and action science, without fetched source text.

source_texts.title: Chris Argyris and Donald A. Schon, Organizational Learning: A Theory of Action Perspective (1978)
Chris Argyris, Reasoning, Learning, and Action: Individual and Organizational (1982)
Chris Argyris, Overcoming Organizational Defenses: Facilitating Organizational Learning (1990)
Peter M. Senge, The Fifth Discipline: The Art and Practice of the Learning Organization (1990)

when_to_use: a recurring error persists even after better procedures, training, monitoring, or incentives have been applied
people can describe the official lesson learned, but behavior under pressure keeps reproducing the same pattern
important facts are treated as embarrassing, disloyal, politically unsafe, or otherwise undiscussable
a team is optimizing to a target that may itself be outdated, harmful, or contradicted by its stated purpose
the gap between stated values and actual decisions is central to the problem
